hero.title: Dyspepsia (Indigestion)
hero.subtitle: Understanding and managing persistent stomach discomfort
hero.description: Everything you need to know about dyspepsia — why your stomach hurts, how your doctor investigates it, what treatments are available, and when to seek help. Clear, trustworthy information to help you feel better.
hero.reassurance: Dyspepsia (indigestion) is extremely common and almost always benign. Most people improve with simple treatment.

sections.title: What Is Dyspepsia?
sections.subtitle: Understanding the different types and causes of indigestion
sections.summary: Dyspepsia is the medical term for indigestion. It refers to pain or discomfort in the upper part of your stomach. It is very common and there are several possible causes, but most are not serious.

content.text: Dyspepsia is the medical word for indigestion. It means pain or discomfort in the upper part of your belly, usually around the area just below your ribs. You might feel a burning sensation, fullness after eating just a small amount, bloating, or nausea. Dyspepsia is one of the most common reasons people visit their doctor — it affects up to 1 in 4 Canadians at some point. The good news is that the cause is almost always something treatable, and serious causes are rare.

points.heading: Very common
points.text: Up to 25% of Canadians experience dyspepsia. You are not alone, and help is available.

points.heading: Usually not serious
points.text: In most cases, dyspepsia is caused by something minor and treatable. Serious causes like stomach cancer are rare, especially in people under 60.

points.heading: Several possible causes
points.text: Dyspepsia can come from a stomach infection (H. pylori), certain medications (like ibuprofen), or simply how your stomach processes food (functional dyspepsia).

points.heading: Treatable
points.text: Whether it is caused by an infection, a medication, or a sensitive stomach, there are effective treatments that help most people feel better.

items.name: Functional dyspepsia
items.description: No visible cause found on testing. The stomach is extra sensitive or does not empty as well as it should. This is the most frequent type and is not dangerous.

items.name: H. pylori-related
items.description: A bacterial infection in the stomach lining that can cause pain, bloating, and nausea. Easily treated with antibiotics.

items.name: NSAID-related
items.description: Pain relievers like ibuprofen (Advil) or naproxen (Aleve) can irritate the stomach lining and cause dyspepsia. Switching medications often helps.

items.name: Structural (ulcer, etc.)
items.description: A stomach ulcer or other visible problem found on endoscopy. Treatable with medication and, in some cases, treating an underlying H. pylori infection.

sections.title: Investigation
sections.subtitle: How your doctor figures out what is causing your symptoms
sections.summary: Your doctor will decide the best approach based on your age, symptoms, and risk factors. Most people do not need an endoscopy right away.

content.text: When you see your doctor about dyspepsia, they will ask about your symptoms, medications, and health history. In Canada, guidelines recommend a "test-and-treat" approach for most people. This means testing for H. pylori first rather than jumping straight to an endoscopy. If you have certain warning signs (called alarm features), your doctor may recommend an endoscopy sooner.

content.heading: The Test-and-Treat Approach (CAG 2021)
steps: Your doctor reviews your symptoms and checks for alarm features (see below)
If no alarm features, you are tested for H. pylori — usually with a simple breath test or stool test
If H. pylori is found, you receive antibiotic treatment to clear the infection
If H. pylori is not found (or symptoms persist after treatment), a trial of acid-lowering medication (PPI) is started for 4 to 8 weeks
If symptoms do not improve with H. pylori treatment or a PPI trial, your doctor may refer you for an upper endoscopy to look more closely

content.title: Alarm features — tell your doctor right away
content.text: Certain symptoms mean your doctor will want to investigate sooner. These include: difficulty swallowing, unintentional weight loss, persistent vomiting, vomiting blood or black stools, anemia (low blood count), or being over 60 with new dyspepsia symptoms. If you have any of these, your doctor may recommend an endoscopy without waiting for a trial of medication.

sections.title: Treatment
sections.subtitle: Options for getting your symptoms under control
sections.summary: Treatment depends on what is causing your dyspepsia. Options include acid-lowering medication, antibiotics for H. pylori, lifestyle changes, and medications that help the stomach work more normally.

content.text: The right treatment depends on the cause of your dyspepsia. If H. pylori is found, antibiotics are the first step. If no clear cause is found (functional dyspepsia), your doctor will work with you to find the medication and lifestyle changes that help most. Treatment is usually very effective, and most people see improvement within a few weeks.

items.note: Reduce stomach acid. Often the first medication tried. Taken once daily before a meal for 4 to 8 weeks.
An alternative acid-reducing option. May be used if PPIs are not suitable or as a step-down from a PPI.
Help the stomach empty more efficiently. Used when bloating or early fullness is the main symptom. Available in Canada by prescription.
Used at very low doses (much lower than for depression) to reduce stomach nerve sensitivity. Can be very helpful for functional dyspepsia.
Helps the stomach relax to hold food more comfortably. Used for functional dyspepsia when fullness and bloating are the main symptoms.
A combination of antibiotics taken for 14 days to clear the H. pylori infection. Your doctor will explain the specific regimen.

content.heading: Lifestyle Changes That Help
items: Eat smaller, more frequent meals rather than large ones
Avoid eating within 3 hours of bedtime
Cut back on fatty and fried foods, which slow stomach emptying
Limit or avoid alcohol and caffeine
If you smoke, talk to your doctor about quitting
Reduce use of NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) — use acetaminophen (Tylenol) instead when possible
Manage stress through exercise, relaxation, or mindfulness — stress can worsen symptoms

content.heading: If you have functional dyspepsia
content.text: Functional dyspepsia means your stomach is extra sensitive even though tests do not show a visible problem. This does not mean your symptoms are imagined — they are real. The nerves in your stomach are simply more reactive than usual. This is a recognized medical condition, and there are effective treatments. Many people improve with a combination of dietary changes, stress management, and medications that calm stomach nerve signals. Your doctor may try more than one approach to find what works best for you.

sections.title: When to Seek Help
sections.subtitle: Symptoms that should prompt a call to your doctor
sections.summary: Most dyspepsia is manageable, but some warning signs mean you should see your doctor promptly.

items: Unintentional weight loss of more than 5 pounds
Persistent vomiting that does not improve
Vomiting blood or material that looks like coffee grounds
Black, tarry stools or blood in your stool
Symptoms not improving after 8 weeks of treatment
New indigestion symptoms starting after age 60
Feeling a lump or mass in your upper belly

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.subtitle: Answers to common questions about dyspepsia
sections.summary: Got questions about your stomach symptoms? Here are clear answers to the things patients ask most often.

questions.q: Is dyspepsia the same as GERD (acid reflux)?
questions.a: Not exactly. GERD mainly causes heartburn and acid coming back up into your throat. Dyspepsia causes pain or discomfort in the upper stomach area, often with bloating, fullness, or nausea. However, the two conditions can overlap, and some people have both. Your doctor can help sort out which one you have.

questions.q: Should I be worried about stomach cancer?
questions.a: Stomach cancer is very rare, especially in people under 60 who do not have alarm features (like weight loss, difficulty swallowing, or bleeding). If you are under 60 with no warning signs, the chance of a serious cause is extremely low. Your doctor will check for alarm features and investigate further if needed.

questions.q: Why did my doctor prescribe an antidepressant for my stomach?
questions.a: Low-dose tricyclic antidepressants (like amitriptyline) are used at doses much lower than those used for depression. At these low doses, they calm the nerve signals in the stomach and reduce pain sensitivity. They are a well-studied treatment for functional dyspepsia and do not mean your doctor thinks your symptoms are caused by depression.

questions.q: Can anxiety or stress cause dyspepsia?
questions.a: Stress and anxiety do not directly cause dyspepsia, but they can make symptoms worse. The gut and brain are closely connected through what doctors call the gut-brain axis. Managing stress through exercise, sleep, relaxation techniques, or counseling can be an important part of treatment.

questions.q: How long will I need to take a PPI?
questions.a: Most people take a PPI for 4 to 8 weeks to see if it helps. If your symptoms improve, your doctor may suggest tapering to the lowest effective dose or trying to stop. Some people need longer treatment, and your doctor will work with you to find the right balance.

It depends on the cause. If dyspepsia is caused by H. pylori, curing the infection often resolves symptoms for good. If it is caused by a medication, switching the medication can help. Functional dyspepsia can come and go over time, but many people find that symptoms improve significantly with treatment and lifestyle changes.

questions.q: Will antacids like Tums or Gaviscon help?
questions.a: Antacids may provide temporary relief, but they are not a long-term solution for most types of dyspepsia. If you find yourself using antacids frequently, it is worth talking to your doctor. A proper assessment can identify the cause and lead to more effective treatment.

questions.q: Are there specific foods I should avoid?
questions.a: There is no single "dyspepsia diet," but many people find that fatty foods, fried foods, spicy foods, caffeine, and alcohol can make symptoms worse. Eating smaller meals and avoiding eating late at night can also help. A food diary can be useful to identify your personal triggers.

sections.title: References
sections.subtitle: Guidelines and resources used in this guide
sections.summary: This guide is based on current Canadian and international gastroenterology guidelines.

items.text: Canadian Association of Gastroenterology — Clinical Practice Guideline on the Management of Uninvestigated Dyspepsia (2021)
American College of Gastroenterology and Canadian Association of Gastroenterology — Clinical Guideline: Management of Dyspepsia (2017)
Rome Foundation — Rome IV Diagnostic Criteria for Functional Gastrointestinal Disorders (2016)
Canadian Digestive Health Foundation — Dyspepsia Patient Information
